name: Reishi
description: Called Lingzhi — the 'mushroom of immortality' — in Taoist tradition, where it was reserved for emperors and immortals. Burned as incense in temples and used in longevity workings for thousands of years.

name: Lion's Mane
description: A mushroom of the mind — used by Buddhist monks to enhance meditation and focus. Associated with the enhancement of the faculties of thought, communication, and inner clarity.

name: Chaga
description: A parasitic fungus grown on birch trees, revered in Siberian shamanic tradition as a spirit of the forest. Associated with Saturn — slow, enduring, protective power drawn from deep roots.

name: Turkey Tail
description: One of the most widespread medicinal fungi in the world, growing on fallen wood as a symbol of transformation and new life from decay. Used in healing workings and rites of renewal.

name: Cordyceps
description: A parasitic fungus of fierce vitality — it overtakes its host and emerges transformed. Used in Tibetan folk medicine as a tonic of supreme endurance, associated with martial will and physical power.

name: Shiitake
description: Cultivated in East Asian tradition as a symbol of longevity and earthly abundance. Used in prosperity workings and as a sacred offering in ancestor rites.

name: Oyster Mushroom
description: A gentle and forgiving mushroom that grows in seemingly inhospitable conditions — a symbol of adaptability and finding nourishment where others see only decay. Used in workings of gentle healing and new growth.

name: Fly Agaric
description: Perhaps the world's most symbolically charged mushroom — linked to Norse berserker warriors, Siberian shamans, the Vedic Soma, and European fairy lore. Its red-and-white cap is the archetypal 'magic mushroom' of Western imagination. Used in shamanic cultures to journey between worlds and commune with spirits.
safety: Toxic when raw — contains ibotenic acid and muscimol. Causes intoxication, delirium, and in high doses serious harm. Legal in most US states but controlled in the UK, Australia, and elsewhere. This entry is for spiritual and historical reference only.

name: Psilocybe
description: Used for millennia in Mesoamerican spiritual practice — the Mazatec curandera María Sabina called them 'children' and used them in all-night healing veladas. The teonanácatl ('flesh of the gods') of Aztec tradition. Now the focus of clinical research into spiritual experience and healing of the psyche.
safety: Schedule I in the US and most countries. Decriminalized in several US cities and states; legal for religious use in limited contexts. Possession and use carry serious legal risk in most jurisdictions. This entry documents historical and spiritual significance only.

name: San Pedro
description: A towering columnar cactus of the Andes — also not a mushroom — used in Andean healing ceremonies (curanderismo) for over 3,000 years. Called huachuma by traditional practitioners. The curandero uses it in all-night mesa ceremonies for healing, cleansing, and connection to Pachamama (Earth Mother) and the spirit world.
safety: The cactus itself is legal to grow in most countries as an ornamental plant. Extracted mescaline is Schedule I in the US. Ceremonial preparation carries legal risk depending on jurisdiction. This entry is for spiritual, historical, and cultural reference only.

name: Salvia Divinorum
description: The 'diviner's sage' — used by the Mazatec people of Oaxaca for healing and divination. Unlike most visionary plants it produces an intensely brief experience. Traditional use involves chewing fresh leaves in darkness and silence, supervised by a curandera, to communicate with a spirit known as the Shepherdess.
safety: Legal in many US states but controlled in others (varies significantly by jurisdiction). Not federally scheduled in the US as of the last update to this data. Legal status changes frequently — verify locally. This entry is for historical and spiritual reference only.
